hook.text: About 4.5 billion years ago, a Mars-sized body slammed into early Earth. The debris became our Moon.

buildup.text: The giant impact hypothesis is the leading theory. The collision vaporized rock, which collected in orbit and cooled.

discovery.text: Apollo samples showed the Moon's composition closely matches Earth's mantle—strong evidence for the impact theory.

twist.text: The impactor, called Theia, may have been absorbed into Earth. Blobs of its material might still exist deep in the mantle.

climax.text: Without the Moon, Earth's axial tilt would wobble wildly. Our Moon stabilizes seasons and may have helped life evolve.

punchline.text: Born from a world-shattering collision. The Moon may be why life thrived here.

quiz.question: What is the leading theory for how Earth's Moon formed?
options: A Mars-sized body collided with early Earth, and debris formed the Moon
The Moon was captured by Earth's gravity as it passed by
The Moon split off from a rapidly spinning young Earth

hook.text: The Moon's surface is covered in craters, dark plains called maria, and bright highlands. Each tells a different story.

buildup.text: The highlands are ancient crust, 4.4 billion years old. The maria are younger lava flows that filled giant impact basins.

discovery.text: The largest basin, South Pole–Aitken, is 2,500 km wide—one of the biggest impact scars in the entire solar system.

twist.text: The near side has many maria; the far side almost none. Thicker far-side crust prevented lava from reaching the surface.

climax.text: Every crater is a record of an ancient impact. The Moon's airless surface preserves them for billions of years.

punchline.text: No air, no erosion. The Moon remembers every hit for billions of years.

quiz.question: Why does the Moon's far side have almost no maria?
options: The crust is thicker there, preventing lava from reaching the surface
The far side was shielded from asteroid impacts by Earth
Solar radiation destroyed the maria on the far side over time

hook.text: There's water ice on the Moon—hidden in permanently shadowed craters near the poles where sunlight never reaches.

buildup.text: Temperatures in these craters drop below –230°C. Ice delivered by comets has survived there for billions of years.

discovery.text: India's Chandrayaan-1 confirmed water molecules on the surface in 2009. NASA's LCROSS mission found ice in a polar crater.

twist.text: This ice could supply future astronauts with drinking water, oxygen, and rocket fuel—making lunar bases practical.

climax.text: NASA's Artemis program and VIPER rover plan to map and study these ice deposits for future use.

punchline.text: Water ice at the poles. It could fuel humanity's return to the Moon.

quiz.question: Why is lunar water ice important for future space exploration?
options: It could provide water, oxygen, and rocket fuel for lunar bases
It proves the Moon once had oceans supporting life
It reflects sunlight and could power solar panels efficiently

hook.text: The Moon causes Earth's ocean tides. In return, Earth is slowly pushing the Moon farther away—about 3.8 cm per year.

buildup.text: Tidal forces raise bulges in Earth's oceans. As Earth rotates, those bulges pull the Moon forward in its orbit.

discovery.text: Apollo astronauts left mirrors on the Moon. Laser ranging measures its distance to within millimeters and confirms the retreat.

twist.text: Billions of years ago, the Moon was much closer. Earth's day was just 6 hours long. Tides were enormous.

climax.text: In the far future, the Moon will be so distant that total solar eclipses will become impossible. We live in a special era.

punchline.text: The Moon drifts away slowly. Total eclipses are a cosmic coincidence of our time.

quiz.question: How do scientists measure the Moon's retreat from Earth?
options: By bouncing lasers off mirrors left by Apollo astronauts on the Moon's surface
By tracking changes in the Moon's brightness over centuries
By measuring shifts in Earth's magnetic field caused by the Moon

topics.title: The Far Side of the Moon

hook.text: We never see the Moon's far side from Earth. Tidal locking keeps one face permanently pointed away from us.

buildup.text: The far side looks completely different—heavily cratered with almost no dark maria. It's rougher and more ancient.

discovery.text: The Soviet Luna 3 took the first photos in 1959. In 2019, China's Chang'e 4 became the first craft to land there.

twist.text: The far side is the quietest place in the inner solar system for radio astronomy—shielded from Earth's radio noise.

climax.text: A radio telescope on the far side could detect signals from the cosmic dark ages—before the first stars formed.

punchline.text: Always hidden from view. The quietest spot near Earth for listening to the cosmos.

quiz.question: Why is the Moon's far side ideal for radio astronomy?
options: It is shielded from Earth's radio noise, making it the quietest nearby location
It receives more sunlight, which powers radio telescopes better
Its craters act as natural satellite dishes for receiving signals

hook.text: Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the solar system—over 400 active volcanoes dot its surface.

buildup.text: Tidal forces from Jupiter and neighboring moons constantly squeeze Io, generating intense internal heat.

discovery.text: Voyager 1 caught an eruption in 1979—the first active volcanism seen beyond Earth. Plumes rose 300 km high.

twist.text: Io's surface is constantly resurfaced by lava flows. There are no impact craters—they're buried under fresh lava.

climax.text: Some eruptions produce lava hotter than anything on Earth today—possibly reaching 1,700°C, similar to early Earth.

punchline.text: Four hundred volcanoes. No craters. The solar system's most restless world.

quiz.question: What makes Io so volcanically active?
options: Tidal forces from Jupiter and neighboring moons generate intense internal heat
Io has a radioactive core that produces far more heat than other moons
Solar radiation heats Io's thin atmosphere, triggering eruptions

hook.text: Europa's smooth, icy surface hides a global ocean of liquid water—twice the volume of all Earth's oceans combined.

buildup.text: The ice shell is perhaps 15–25 km thick. Below it, a salt-water ocean extends down to a rocky seafloor.

discovery.text: Galileo spacecraft data revealed an induced magnetic field—a signature of a conductive liquid (salt water) beneath the ice.

twist.text: Reddish-brown streaks on the surface may be salts from the ocean below—delivered by cryovolcanism or ice convection.

climax.text: Europa is the top candidate for finding extraterrestrial life in our solar system. The ocean has water, heat, and chemistry.

punchline.text: Twice Earth's water, under a shell of ice. The ocean most likely to harbor alien life.

quiz.question: How did scientists confirm Europa has a subsurface ocean?
options: The Galileo spacecraft detected an induced magnetic field from conductive salt water
They observed liquid water erupting from large surface cracks
Infrared telescopes measured heat rising from under the ice

hook.text: Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system—bigger than Mercury. It has its own magnetic field.

buildup.text: It's about 5,270 km across. Half its surface is old and cratered; the other half has younger grooved terrain.

discovery.text: Ganymede is the only moon known to generate its own magnetic field—produced by a liquid iron core deep inside.

twist.text: Hubble observations suggest a subsurface ocean sandwiched between layers of ice, perhaps 100 km below the surface.

climax.text: ESA's JUICE mission will orbit Ganymede in the 2030s—the first spacecraft to orbit a moon other than our own.

punchline.text: Bigger than Mercury. Its own magnetic field. An ocean hiding in the deep.

quiz.question: What makes Ganymede unique among all moons in the solar system?
options: It is the only moon known to generate its own magnetic field
It is the only moon larger than a planet
It has the most volcanically active surface of any moon

hook.text: Callisto is the most heavily cratered body in the solar system. Its surface hasn't changed much in 4 billion years.

buildup.text: It orbits far enough from Jupiter to avoid strong tidal heating. Without internal activity, its surface stays frozen in time.

discovery.text: Valhalla is its largest impact basin—a 3,800 km ring system from an ancient collision. It dominates one hemisphere.

twist.text: Despite its dead surface, Callisto may have a subsurface ocean too—detected through magnetic field measurements.

climax.text: Its low radiation environment makes it the safest Galilean moon for a future human base to explore Jupiter's system.

punchline.text: Ancient, battered, and quiet. But possibly hiding an ocean—and a future base.

quiz.question: Why would Callisto be the safest Galilean moon for a human base?
options: It orbits far from Jupiter and has a low radiation environment
It has a thick atmosphere that shields against cosmic rays
Its surface has no craters or rough terrain to complicate landings

hook.text: In 1610, Galileo pointed his telescope at Jupiter and saw four tiny lights moving around it. They were moons.

buildup.text: He tracked Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto over weeks. Their orbits proved that not everything revolved around Earth.

discovery.text: This was the first evidence that celestial bodies could orbit something other than Earth—a blow to geocentrism.

twist.text: The Catholic Church pressured Galileo to recant. He spent his final years under house arrest for supporting Copernicus.

climax.text: Today Jupiter has 95 known moons. But those first four changed how humanity understood the universe forever.

punchline.text: Four moons through a simple telescope. They toppled Earth from the center of the cosmos.

quiz.question: Why was Galileo's discovery of Jupiter's moons so revolutionary?
options: It proved that celestial bodies could orbit something other than Earth
It was the first evidence that the Sun orbits the Earth
It showed that moons could support life

hook.text: Titan is the only moon with a thick atmosphere—denser than Earth's. It has rain, rivers, and lakes of liquid methane.

buildup.text: Its nitrogen-rich atmosphere is four times denser than ours at the surface. You could strap on wings and fly there.

discovery.text: The Huygens probe landed on Titan in 2005—the farthest landing from Earth ever. It sent back images of a misty orange world.

twist.text: Titan's methane cycle mirrors Earth's water cycle. Clouds form, rain falls, rivers carve channels, and lakes collect the liquid.

climax.text: Complex organic molecules form in the atmosphere. Titan may resemble early Earth—a prebiotic laboratory waiting to be studied.

punchline.text: Rain, rivers, and lakes—all methane. The most Earth-like world, in the strangest way.

quiz.question: What liquid forms Titan's rain, rivers, and lakes?
options: Methane and ethane
Liquid water
Liquid nitrogen

hook.text: Tiny Enceladus—just 500 km across—shoots geysers of salty water from cracks near its south pole into space.

buildup.text: Cassini flew through the plumes multiple times. It found water, salt, organic molecules, and molecular hydrogen.

discovery.text: The hydrogen suggests hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor—the same energy source that supports life in Earth's deep oceans.

twist.text: Enceladus is so small that it shouldn't have enough heat for an ocean. Tidal interactions with Saturn provide the extra energy.

climax.text: A future plume-diving mission could analyze the water for biosignatures—sampling an alien ocean without landing.

punchline.text: A tiny moon shooting free ocean samples into space. We just need to go catch them.

quiz.question: Why does Enceladus have a subsurface ocean despite its tiny size?
options: Tidal interactions with Saturn generate enough heat to keep water liquid
Its core is made entirely of radioactive materials that produce heat
The geysers are powered by chemical reactions, not internal heat

hook.text: Mimas has a giant crater named Herschel that makes it look just like the Death Star from Star Wars.

buildup.text: The crater is 130 km wide—nearly a third of the moon's diameter. The impact nearly shattered Mimas entirely.

discovery.text: Voyager 1 photographed it in 1980. The resemblance to the Death Star was immediately noticed—and became iconic.

twist.text: In 2024, Cassini data revealed Mimas likely hides a young subsurface ocean—the last place anyone expected to find one.

climax.text: If tiny, cratered Mimas has an ocean, subsurface water may be far more common in the solar system than anyone thought.

punchline.text: Looks like the Death Star. Might hide a secret ocean. Full of surprises.

quiz.question: What surprising discovery was made about Mimas in 2024?
options: Cassini data revealed it likely hides a young subsurface ocean
It was found to have active volcanoes under its ice surface
Scientists detected a magnetic field stronger than any other moon

hook.text: Iapetus is split in two—one hemisphere is bright white ice, the other is dark as coal. The contrast is dramatic.

buildup.text: Giovanni Cassini noticed in 1671 that Iapetus vanished on one side of its orbit. The dark side was too faint to see.

discovery.text: The dark material comes from dust swept up from an outer Saturnian moon. It coats the leading hemisphere like soot.

twist.text: Sunlight warms the dark side, sublimating ice. The ice migrates to the bright side, making each half more extreme over time.

climax.text: Iapetus also has a mysterious equatorial ridge 20 km high—taller than Everest. Its origin is still debated.

punchline.text: Half black, half white, with a ridge taller than Everest. A moon of extremes.

quiz.question: Why is one hemisphere of Iapetus much darker than the other?
options: Dust from an outer moon coats the leading side, and thermal feedback makes the contrast grow
Volcanic eruptions deposited dark lava on only one side
The dark side faces the Sun more often, causing chemical changes in the ice

hook.text: Saturn's moons and rings are locked in a gravitational dance. Small moons sculpt the rings into sharp edges and gaps.

buildup.text: Shepherd moons like Prometheus and Pandora keep the F ring narrow. Their gravity corrals ring particles into place.

discovery.text: Pan orbits inside the Encke Gap and clears it by sweeping up ring particles. It's shaped like a flying saucer from the buildup.

twist.text: Some tiny moonlets exist within the rings themselves—barely visible, only revealed by the wakes they leave in ring material.

climax.text: Cassini's Grand Finale dived between the rings and Saturn. It found that the rings may be only 100 million years old.

punchline.text: Moons shape the rings. The rings feed the moons. A system in constant exchange.

quiz.question: What do shepherd moons like Prometheus and Pandora do?
options: Their gravity corrals ring particles, keeping ring edges sharp and narrow
They generate Saturn's magnetic field through their orbital motion
They pull ring material away from Saturn and into deep space

hook.text: Miranda is a tiny moon of Uranus with one of the most bizarre surfaces in the solar system—a jumble of mismatched terrains.

buildup.text: It has enormous canyons 20 km deep, chevron-shaped ridges, and smooth plains all stitched together randomly.

discovery.text: Voyager 2 flew past in 1986 and captured images that stunned scientists. No one expected such a small moon to look so wild.

twist.text: One theory says Miranda was shattered by a collision and reassembled—pieces falling back together in a random patchwork.

climax.text: Another theory suggests tidal heating drove geological activity, pushing different icy materials to the surface.

punchline.text: A patchwork of cliffs and canyons. Shattered and reassembled, or heated from within.

quiz.question: What makes Miranda's surface so unusual?
options: It has mismatched terrains with enormous canyons, ridges, and plains stitched together randomly
It is perfectly smooth with no craters or surface features at all
It is covered in active volcanoes erupting liquid water

hook.text: Triton orbits Neptune backward—the only large moon with a retrograde orbit. It was likely captured from the Kuiper Belt.

buildup.text: It's one of the coldest surfaces known: –235°C. Nitrogen ice covers the surface, and geysers blast nitrogen gas upward.

discovery.text: Voyager 2 found active geysers in 1989—dark plumes rising 8 km high, blown sideways by thin winds.

twist.text: Triton's retrograde orbit means tidal forces are slowly shrinking it. In billions of years, it will be torn apart into a ring.

climax.text: Triton may have a subsurface ocean. A proposed Trident mission would search for signs of geological activity and water.

punchline.text: A captured world orbiting backward. Geysers, ice, and a doomed orbit ending in a ring.

quiz.question: Why do scientists believe Triton was captured by Neptune?
options: It orbits in the opposite direction to Neptune's rotation, suggesting it didn't form there
Its chemical composition matches asteroids from the inner solar system
It has no craters, indicating it arrived recently in cosmic terms

hook.text: Uranus has two similar-sized moons—Ariel and Umbriel—but their surfaces tell opposite stories.

buildup.text: Ariel is bright and geologically active, with deep canyons and smooth plains suggesting past resurfacing.

discovery.text: Umbriel is dark and ancient, covered in craters with almost no signs of geological activity at all.

twist.text: Why they differ so much is a mystery. Their sizes and orbits are similar, yet their histories diverged dramatically.

climax.text: Only Voyager 2 has visited—briefly, in 1986. A future Uranus mission could finally explain these contrasting twins.

punchline.text: Same size, same planet, opposite stories. We visited once, 40 years ago.

quiz.question: What makes Ariel and Umbriel puzzling as a pair?
options: Despite similar sizes and orbits, one is geologically active and the other is ancient and cratered
They orbit Uranus in opposite directions despite being the same size
One is made of rock and the other entirely of ice

hook.text: Uranus spins on its side, tilted 98 degrees. Its moons orbit in the same tilted plane—like a giant spinning top.

buildup.text: This means the moons' poles alternately face the Sun for decades at a time. Seasons are extreme and decades long.

discovery.text: A giant impact likely knocked Uranus onto its side early in solar system history. The moons formed from the resulting debris.

twist.text: Uranus has 28 known moons. All are named after characters from Shakespeare and Alexander Pope—unique in the solar system.

climax.text: The Planetary Decadal Survey ranked a Uranus orbiter as the top priority flagship mission for the coming decade.

punchline.text: A sideways planet with Shakespearean moons. Our top target for the next big mission.

quiz.question: Why do Uranus's moons orbit in a tilted plane?
options: A giant impact likely knocked Uranus on its side, and the moons formed in that plane
Strong magnetic fields from Uranus pull the moons into unusual orbits
The moons were captured from passing asteroids at different angles

hook.text: Neptune has 16 known moons, but Triton dominates. The rest are tiny, dark, and largely mysterious.

buildup.text: Nereid has one of the most eccentric orbits of any moon—swinging from close to Neptune out to enormous distances.

discovery.text: Hippocamp, discovered in 2013, is just 34 km across. It may be a fragment blasted off the larger moon Proteus by an impact.

twist.text: Triton's capture likely destroyed or ejected Neptune's original moons. The current small moons formed from the debris afterward.

climax.text: Only Voyager 2 has visited Neptune. Most moons were discovered by Earth telescopes and Hubble after the flyby.

punchline.text: Tiny survivors of Triton's capture. A system reshaped by one dramatic arrival.

quiz.question: How did Triton's capture affect Neptune's original moon system?
options: It likely destroyed or ejected the original moons, and current small moons formed from debris
It had no effect—Neptune's moons were already in their current orbits
Triton absorbed all the original moons during its capture

topics.title: Phobos and Deimos: Mars's Tiny Moons

hook.text: Mars has two tiny moons—Phobos and Deimos. They look like lumpy potatoes, just 22 km and 12 km across.

buildup.text: Both are dark and heavily cratered. They may be captured asteroids, or debris from a giant impact on Mars.

discovery.text: Asaph Hall discovered them in 1877. Phobos orbits so close to Mars that it rises and sets twice per Martian day.

twist.text: Phobos is spiraling inward. In about 50 million years, it will either crash into Mars or be torn apart into a ring.

climax.text: Japan's MMX mission will visit Phobos in the 2020s, land on its surface, and return samples to Earth.

punchline.text: Two potato-shaped moons. One is doomed to become a ring around Mars.

quiz.question: What will eventually happen to Phobos?
options: It will crash into Mars or be torn apart into a ring in about 50 million years
It will escape Mars's gravity and become an independent asteroid
It will collide with Deimos and both moons will be destroyed

hook.text: Charon is so large relative to Pluto that they orbit each other—a true binary system, not a planet and moon.

buildup.text: Charon is half Pluto's size. They're tidally locked to each other—the same face always points toward the partner.

discovery.text: New Horizons revealed Charon in 2015—a world with canyons, mountains, and a mysterious dark red polar cap.

twist.text: The red cap is likely methane from Pluto's atmosphere that froze on Charon's pole and was darkened by radiation.

climax.text: Charon likely formed from a giant impact—similar to Earth's Moon. Pluto's system may mirror our own origin story.

punchline.text: Half as big as its planet. They dance together, locked face to face forever.

quiz.question: Why are Pluto and Charon considered a binary system?
options: Charon is so large relative to Pluto that they orbit a shared point between them
They were once a single body that split into two equal halves
Both objects have the same surface composition and density

hook.text: Even asteroids have moons. Over 400 asteroids are known to have one or more tiny companions orbiting them.

buildup.text: Dactyl was the first asteroid moon discovered in 1993, orbiting the asteroid Ida. Galileo snapped the image during a flyby.

discovery.text: Asteroid Dinkinesh, visited by Lucy in 2023, surprised everyone with a contact-binary moon—two tiny rocks touching.

twist.text: Asteroid moons form from collisions, spin-off, or gravitational capture. Some binary asteroids spin so fast they nearly fly apart.

climax.text: DART proved we can deflect asteroids by hitting Dimorphos—the moon of asteroid Didymos. Planetary defense works.

punchline.text: Even rocks have moons. We've already redirected one for planetary defense.

quiz.question: What did NASA's DART mission accomplish at the asteroid Didymos?
options: It successfully deflected the asteroid's moon Dimorphos, proving planetary defense is possible
It landed on the asteroid and collected samples for return to Earth
It discovered that the asteroid had water ice on its surface

hook.text: Many objects in the Kuiper Belt have moons. Some are binary pairs that orbit each other as equals.

buildup.text: Pluto has five moons. Eris has Dysnomia. Haumea has two. Even small KBOs often have companions.

discovery.text: New Horizons visited Arrokoth in 2019—a contact binary that looks like a snowman. Two objects gently merged long ago.

twist.text: Binary KBOs probably formed together from the same cloud of material, rather than one capturing the other.

climax.text: Studying KBO binaries reveals conditions in the early solar system—they've been frozen in time for 4.5 billion years.

punchline.text: Frozen pairs at the edge of the solar system. Time capsules from the very beginning.

quiz.question: Why are Kuiper Belt Object binaries scientifically valuable?
options: They have remained largely unchanged for 4.5 billion years, preserving early solar system conditions
They produce unique radiation that reveals the composition of the outer solar system
They are the only objects large enough to be studied with current telescopes

hook.text: Most giant planets have dozens of small, distant moons on tilted or backward orbits. These are captured objects.

buildup.text: Jupiter has 95 known moons, Saturn 146. Most are irregulars—just a few kilometers wide, orbiting far from the planet.

discovery.text: Modern surveys keep finding more. Saturn surpassed Jupiter's moon count in 2023 when 62 new tiny moons were confirmed.

twist.text: Many irregulars form families—groups with similar orbits, likely fragments from a single captured object that broke apart.

climax.text: These moons are windows into the small-body population of the early solar system—captured billions of years ago.

punchline.text: Hundreds of captured moons. Broken fragments of ancient wanderers, still orbiting.

quiz.question: What are irregular moons?
options: Small moons on tilted or backward orbits, likely captured from elsewhere in the solar system
Moons that change shape as they orbit due to tidal forces
Moons that orbit in the exact same plane as their planet's rings

hook.text: Between 1969 and 1972, twelve humans walked on the Moon. They brought back 382 kg of rocks that transformed lunar science.

buildup.text: Apollo 11 was the first landing. Neil Armstrong's 'one small step' was watched by 600 million people worldwide.

discovery.text: Apollo samples proved the Moon formed from a giant impact. They also revealed the Moon's volcanic past and true age.

twist.text: Apollo 17 geologist Harrison Schmitt found orange soil—tiny glass beads from an ancient volcanic eruption 3.7 billion years ago.

climax.text: No human has returned since 1972. Artemis aims to change that—landing the first woman and first person of color on the Moon.

punchline.text: Twelve people, six landings, 382 kg of Moon rocks. The greatest exploration feat so far.

quiz.question: What did Apollo moon rocks reveal about the Moon's origin?
options: They proved the Moon formed from a giant impact with early Earth
They showed the Moon captured water from passing comets
They confirmed the Moon was once part of Mars

hook.text: Cassini orbited Saturn for 13 years, transforming our understanding of its rings, moons, and atmosphere.

buildup.text: It arrived in 2004 and made groundbreaking discoveries at Titan, Enceladus, and within Saturn's ring system.

discovery.text: Huygens landed on Titan in 2005, revealing river channels and a methane-soaked landscape beneath orange haze.

twist.text: In its Grand Finale, Cassini dived between Saturn and its rings 22 times—getting closer than any spacecraft ever had.

climax.text: Cassini was deliberately crashed into Saturn in 2017 to protect Enceladus from contamination. It sent data until the end.

punchline.text: Thirteen years at Saturn. Crashed to protect a moon that might have life.

quiz.question: Why was Cassini deliberately crashed into Saturn at the end of its mission?
options: To prevent it from contaminating Enceladus, which might harbor life
Its fuel ran out and it could no longer maintain orbit
Scientists wanted to study Saturn's atmosphere by sending Cassini through it

hook.text: Europa Clipper launched in 2024 on a mission to determine if Europa's ocean could support life.

buildup.text: It will make about 50 close flybys, mapping the ice shell with radar and analyzing surface chemistry with spectrometers.

discovery.text: The spacecraft carries nine instruments designed to study Europa's ice, ocean, composition, and geology in detail.

twist.text: Clipper won't land or enter orbit around Europa—Jupiter's radiation is too intense. Quick flybys minimize exposure.

climax.text: If Clipper confirms a habitable ocean, a lander mission will follow—possibly the mission that detects alien life.

punchline.text: Fifty passes over an alien ocean. Looking for the conditions that life requires.

quiz.question: Why does Europa Clipper use flybys instead of orbiting Europa directly?
options: Jupiter's intense radiation field makes prolonged orbits around Europa too dangerous
Europa's gravity is too weak to maintain a stable orbit
The spacecraft needs to visit other moons between each Europa pass

hook.text: ESA's JUICE mission launched in 2023 to study Jupiter's three icy moons: Ganymede, Europa, and Callisto.

buildup.text: After arriving in 2031, it will perform multiple flybys of all three moons before entering orbit around Ganymede.

discovery.text: It will be the first spacecraft to orbit a moon other than Earth's—a milestone in planetary exploration.

twist.text: JUICE will probe Ganymede's magnetic field, subsurface ocean, and ice shell—searching for conditions that could support life.

climax.text: Combined with Europa Clipper, JUICE will give us the most complete picture ever of Jupiter's ocean worlds.

punchline.text: Three icy moons, one orbiter. The first mission to orbit another world's moon.

quiz.question: What milestone will JUICE achieve at Ganymede?
options: It will be the first spacecraft to orbit a moon other than Earth's Moon
It will be the first mission to land on an icy moon's surface
It will be the first spacecraft to enter Jupiter's atmosphere

hook.text: The coming decades will see more moon missions than ever. From our Moon to Europa, Titan, and Enceladus—the era has begun.

buildup.text: Artemis will establish a permanent human presence on the Moon. Gateway station will orbit it as a stepping stone.

discovery.text: Dragonfly will fly on Titan. A proposed Enceladus Orbilander would orbit then land—tasting the ocean plumes for life.

twist.text: Private companies are joining. SpaceX, ispace, and others plan lunar landers and rovers of their own.

climax.text: Each mission answers old questions and raises new ones. The solar system's moons may hold the biggest discovery of all.

punchline.text: Our Moon, then ocean moons. The next giant leap might find life among them.

quiz.question: What is the proposed Enceladus Orbilander mission designed to do?
options: Orbit Enceladus and then land to sample its ocean plumes for signs of life
Orbit Saturn and observe Enceladus from a safe distance
Land on Enceladus and drill through the ice to reach the ocean directly
